style.name: spacing
style.description: A spatial system built on a 4px base unit. Defines the scale and rules for all whitespace, padding, margin, and gap values across the system.

principles.title: Use the scale
principles.description: Every spacing value in production code must reference a token from the spacing scale. Hard-coded pixel, rem, or em values are not permitted. The scale exists to constrain decisions to a set of values that are proven to produce consistent rhythm.

principles.title: Density over decoration
principles.description: Spacing exists to create clear relationships between elements — not to fill empty areas. Tighter spacing signals that elements are related. Wider spacing signals separation. Choose spacing based on the relationship between elements, not on what looks good in isolation.

principles.title: Consistent internal, flexible external
principles.description: Internal spacing within a component (padding, gaps between sub-elements) is fixed by the component's design specification. External spacing between components is determined by the layout context and may vary. Components do not own their own margins.

principles.title: Optical alignment over mathematical alignment
principles.description: In rare cases, mathematically equal spacing can appear visually unequal due to the shape of adjacent elements (e.g., icons next to text, rounded containers). When this occurs, the nearest scale value that produces optical alignment is preferred. Document the exception.

whenToUse.description: When defining padding, margin, or gap values for any layout or component. All spatial values in production code must reference the spacing scale.
When establishing vertical rhythm between content sections, form groups, or card layouts.
When setting internal padding for containers such as cards, dialogs, panels, and page regions.
When controlling the gap between sibling elements in Flexbox or Grid layouts.

whenNotToUse.description: When defining border widths, outline offsets, or stroke values.

alternative.name: border-width
alternative.rationale: Border tokens are tuned for visual weight at sub-pixel and single-pixel sizes. Spacing tokens start at 0px and jump to values optimized for whitespace, which produce incorrect visual results when applied to borders.

whenNotToUse.description: When setting font sizes or line heights.

alternative.name: typography
alternative.rationale: Typographic tokens follow a modular scale designed for readability and vertical rhythm. Spacing tokens follow a geometric scale designed for whitespace. The two scales serve different purposes and should not be interchanged.

whenNotToUse.description: When sizing icons or illustration containers.

alternative.name: icon-size
alternative.rationale: Icon size tokens are calibrated to optical alignment with adjacent text at each type scale step. Spacing tokens do not account for optical sizing and produce misaligned icons.

whenNotToUse.description: When a precise pixel value is required for a one-off alignment adjustment that does not correspond to any scale step.

alternative.name: spacing-exception
alternative.rationale: Document the exception with a code comment explaining why no scale value applies. One-off values should be rare and reviewed during design critiques.

guidelines.guidance: Use the spacing scale tokens for all padding, margin, and gap properties. Do not use hard-coded values.
guidelines.rationale: Hard-coded values create visual inconsistency and are not responsive to system-wide spacing changes. Tokens enable global adjustment of spatial density from a single source of truth.

examples.description: Uses the spacing token for consistent padding.
Hard-coded value bypasses the spacing system and will not respond to system-wide density changes.

guidelines.guidance: Components must not apply external margin. Margin between components is the responsibility of the parent layout.
guidelines.rationale: Components that own their margins create unpredictable spacing when composed. A button with built-in margin-bottom behaves differently depending on what follows it. Delegating margin to layout (via gap, stack components, or parent padding) makes spacing composable and predictable.

examples.description: Spacing between buttons is controlled by the parent Stack layout.
The button owns its external spacing. This creates problems when the button is the last child or appears in a horizontal layout.

guidelines.guidance: Use space-0 (0px) explicitly when two elements must share an edge with no gap. Do not omit the spacing value.
guidelines.rationale: Explicit zero spacing communicates intent. Omitting a spacing value is ambiguous — the reader cannot tell whether zero spacing was intentional or whether the author forgot to add it.

guidelines.guidance: Select spacing based on the relationship between elements, not their size. Related elements use smaller spacing. Unrelated elements use larger spacing.
guidelines.rationale: Gestalt proximity principle: objects that are closer together are perceived as related. Spacing encodes information hierarchy. A label 4px from its input is clearly associated with it. A label 32px from an input appears disconnected.

examples.description: Spacing increases with decreasing relatedness. Three levels of spatial hierarchy.

presentation.code: Label to input: space-2 (4px). Input group to next input group: space-5 (16px). Form section to form section: space-7 (32px).

examples.description: Uniform spacing flattens hierarchy. The reader cannot distinguish related groups from unrelated ones.

presentation.code: Label to input: space-5 (16px). Input group to next input group: space-5 (16px). Form section to form section: space-5 (16px).

guidelines.guidance: When laying out text, vertical spacing between typographic elements should be relative to the text's line height, not a fixed pixel value. Use spacing tokens that pair with the typographic scale.
guidelines.rationale: Text with a 24px line height needs different surrounding spacing than text with a 16px line height to produce the same visual rhythm. Pairing spacing with the typographic scale ensures consistent proportional whitespace.

guidelines.guidance: Do not use spacing tokens for non-spatial properties such as border-width, font-size, or icon-size. Use the tokens designated for those properties.
guidelines.rationale: Spacing tokens are tuned for whitespace. A spacing scale step that works well as padding produces incorrect results as a border-width or icon-size. Using the wrong token category couples unrelated visual properties, causing cascading regressions when the spacing scale is adjusted.

guidelines.guidance: For responsive layouts, reduce spacing density at smaller breakpoints by stepping down the scale. Do not introduce arbitrary breakpoint-specific values.
guidelines.rationale: Stepping down the scale (e.g., space-7 at desktop becoming space-5 at mobile) maintains proportional relationships while conserving space. Arbitrary values break out of the scale and undermine system consistency.

examples.description: Both values are on the scale. The ratio between section and element spacing is preserved.

presentation.code: Section gap: space-8 (48px) at desktop → space-6 (24px) at mobile.

examples.description: 18px is not on the scale. The relationship to other spacing values is unpredictable.

presentation.code: Section gap: space-8 (48px) at desktop → 18px at mobile.

guidelines.guidance: Use CSS gap (in Flexbox or Grid) as the primary mechanism for spacing between sibling elements. Reserve margin for spacing between non-sibling elements or layout-level offset.
guidelines.rationale: Gap applies spacing uniformly between children without affecting the first or last child. Margin requires :first-child/:last-child overrides to prevent unwanted space at container edges. Gap produces cleaner, more maintainable code.

guidelines.guidance: Ensure all interactive elements maintain a minimum 44x44 CSS pixel touch target, inclusive of padding. If the visible element is smaller, extend the tap target area using padding or pseudo-elements.
guidelines.rationale: Users with motor impairments and touch device users require a minimum target size to interact reliably. WCAG 2.5.8 requires 24x24px minimum and recommends 44x44px. The spacing system's role is to ensure padding contributes to meeting this target.

guidelines.guidance: Do not reduce spacing below space-2 (4px) between interactive elements.
guidelines.rationale: Interactive elements placed too close together increase the risk of accidental activation. A minimum 4px gap between adjacent clickable/tappable elements reduces mis-taps for users with motor impairments.

guidelines.guidance: Maintain sufficient spacing around text content so that it does not touch container edges. Minimum internal padding for text containers is space-3 (8px).
guidelines.rationale: Text that touches its container edge is harder to read for users with cognitive disabilities and low vision. Padding provides a buffer zone that improves legibility and reduces visual crowding.

guidelines.guidance: When users increase text size to 200% via browser settings, spacing must not collapse to zero or cause content to overlap.
guidelines.rationale: Users with low vision enlarge text. If spacing is defined in fixed pixel units that do not scale, text enlargement causes overlap and loss of content. Use rem-based spacing tokens where possible, and test all layouts at 200% text zoom.

tokenGroups.name: spacing
tokenGroups.role: The core spacing token group. Contains all spatial tokens from space-0 (0px) through space-12 (128px), built on a 4px base unit.

scales.name: spacing-scale
scales.description: A geometric spacing scale built on a 4px base unit. Values double at regular intervals to produce a natural rhythm. Use these tokens for all padding, margin, and gap properties. The scale is intentionally constrained — if a needed value is not on the scale, reconsider the design before requesting a new token.

steps.description: Zero spacing. Use when two elements must share an edge. Always declare explicitly to communicate intent.

steps.label: 4xs
steps.description: Hairline spacing. Reserved for border offsets and sub-pixel adjustments. Do not use for layout spacing.

steps.label: 3xs
steps.description: Minimal spacing. Use for tight optical adjustments, such as aligning an icon to text baseline.

steps.label: 2xs
steps.description: Extra-small spacing. Use for the gap between an icon and its adjacent label, or between tightly coupled inline elements.

steps.label: xs
steps.description: Small spacing. Use for internal padding in compact components (badges, chips, tags) and the gap between a form label and its input.

steps.label: sm
steps.description: Small-medium spacing. Use for internal padding in medium-density components and the gap between related form fields.

steps.label: md
steps.description: Base spacing. The default for internal component padding (buttons, inputs, cards) and the gap between sibling elements in a standard layout. This is the most frequently used value.

steps.label: lg
steps.description: Large spacing. Use for padding inside containers (cards, dialogs, panels) and the gap between distinct content groups within a surface.

steps.label: xl
steps.description: Extra-large spacing. Use for the gap between sections within a page or between unrelated content groups.

steps.label: 2xl
steps.description: Double extra-large spacing. Use for major section breaks on desktop layouts.

steps.label: 3xl
steps.description: Triple extra-large spacing. Use for page-level vertical rhythm between hero sections or major content regions.

steps.label: 4xl
steps.description: Maximum layout spacing. Use for top-level page padding on wide viewports or spacing between full-bleed content sections.

steps.label: 5xl
steps.description: Maximum layout spacing. Reserved for landing page hero margins and full-bleed layout padding on extra-wide viewports.
